summary: Product-visible ads need a repeated visual anchor so generated clips feel like one campaign rather than unrelated shots.
principles: Give the product one recurring hero angle, color cue, or interaction motif that appears in every product-visible scene.
Repeat the visual anchor at hook, proof, and CTA with increasing clarity — the product should feel like it's arriving, not appearing randomly.
Preserve geometry, logo placement, material cues, and proportions across every generated shot — inconsistency destroys credibility.
The product's first appearance should be the most visually striking — subsequent appearances reinforce, not re-introduce.
Environmental context should change between scenes but product treatment should remain constant — same lighting quality, same angle bias, same visual weight.
If the product has a signature feature (camera bump, notch, curve), that feature should be visible in every hero shot as a recognition anchor.
Product continuity extends to the CTA frame: the final product shot should be the cleanest, most confident rendering in the entire ad.
apply_when: The ad shows a physical product, package, device, app UI, or recognizable brand asset.
The production uses generated video where product identity drift is likely.
The campaign depends on premium product memory.
avoid_when: The product is intentionally abstract or invisible.
The user accepts a risk waiver for generic concept visuals.
The ad is only a typography or voice-led announcement.
failure_patterns: Product looks different in every scene: different proportions, color, or material quality across generated clips — the viewer questions if it's the same product.
Product visible but not heroed: the product is in the frame but not the visual focus, making the ad feel like a lifestyle video that happens to include a product.
Reference image drift: generated scenes gradually move away from the original product reference, especially after multiple generation rounds.
Logo or brand mark inconsistent: rotated, resized, or repositioned between scenes in a way that looks careless.

cross_domain_notes.note: Consistent camera angle across product-visible scenes reinforces continuity — the viewer's eye recognizes the product by its silhouette before processing details.
Product color must remain consistent across scenes even as the background palette shifts — the product is the color anchor.
Match cuts between product-visible scenes create visual continuity even when the environment changes around the product.

execution_techniques: Define the hero angle in the scene plan: '3/4 left, slight overhead, product filling 60% of frame' — this gives every generation prompt a consistent base.
Extract a product keyframe from the reference image and compare it against every generated clip's first frame before accepting.
Lock the product's color values (hex codes) in the production bible and include them in every generation prompt.
Use the same light direction on the product across all scenes: if the key light is from upper-left in the hero, it stays upper-left in the proof.
